summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Amazon's Mechanical Turk service is failing because it relies on monetary rewards for tasks that would be better motivated intrinsically. He observes that almost two years after launch, the available tasks are largely spammy, poorly compensated, and require excessive oversight. Atwood contrasts this with Amazon's own successful unpaid user review system and other platforms like Folding@Home, Digg, and Reddit where people contribute valuable work without payment. Drawing on Mary Poppendieck's research on compensation, he suggests that extrinsic monetary rewards actually destroy intrinsic motivation over time, and that choosing payment as a reward model may be an irreversible decision that doomed Mechanical Turk from the start.
key_points: Mechanical Turk tasks are predominantly low-paying ($0.10), spammy, and require subjective human grading
Amazon ironically built its business on unpaid user reviews while struggling to make paid microtasks work
Intrinsic motivation drives people to contribute to Folding@Home, Digg, Reddit, and social networks without payment
Extrinsic rewards like money destroy intrinsic motivation over time according to compensation research
Once you introduce monetary rewards, you may never be able to return to intrinsic motivation models
money_quotes: What I find ironic about Amazon's Mechanical Turk service is that Amazon built an entire business around the value of user reviews.
That's user-submitted content that people invested countless thousands of man-hours on. And Amazon didn't pay anyone a dime to do it.
I think the secret to running a viable Mechanical Turk service is, paradoxically, to do away with payment.
the rates make working in a sweatshop seem lucrative
Many studies have shown that extrinsic rewards like grades and pay will, over time, destroy the intrinsic reward that comes from the work itself.
once you go down the path of monetary rewards, you may never be able to go back, even when they cease to be effective, as they inevitably will.
key_insight: Paying people small amounts for microtasks may actually be worse than not paying them at all, because monetary rewards destroy the intrinsic motivation that drives successful crowdsourced platforms.
